insights.title: Bank credit at ₹212.1L Cr — first decline in 6 periods
insights.body: Total bank credit fell to ₹212.1 lakh crore in April 2026, down ₹1.5 lakh crore from the March peak of ₹213.6 lakh crore. Volume contracted ₹213.6L Cr → ₹212.1L Cr, the first month-on-month decline after five consecutive months of expansion from ₹178.7L Cr in January 2025.
insights.implication: This is the first reversal in the upward trajectory since the dataset began — watch whether this is a one-month correction or the start of a cooling cycle. Banks remain near record deployment levels, but momentum has broken.

insights.title: FY26 acceleration jumps to 7.1pp — new record high
insights.body: Bank credit growth accelerated 7.1 percentage points from FY25 to FY26, the highest acceleration in this dataset. Acceleration surged from 5.1pp → 7.1pp, a 2.0 point jump in a single period after holding flat at 5.1pp for six consecutive months.
insights.implication: The structural shift into a higher growth regime has intensified — this is not just sustained momentum but a sharp re-acceleration. Banks are deploying capital faster than at any point in this window, signaling either strong demand or aggressive supply-side push.

insights.title: FY26 credit addition hits ₹31.2L Cr — largest on record
insights.body: Banks added ₹31.2 lakh crore in absolute credit during FY26, the maximum annual expansion in this dataset. The FY26 addition rose from ₹29.6L Cr → ₹31.2L Cr, an increase of ₹1.6 lakh crore over the prior reading.
insights.implication: This is the largest single-year credit creation on record, surpassing the prior peak by ₹1.6 lakh crore. Banks have scaled up lending capacity and are deploying it at unprecedented levels — a clear signal of institutional confidence or policy-driven expansion.

insights.title: Bank credit YoY holds at 16.0% — near upper range
insights.body: Bank credit grew 16.0% year-on-year in April 2026, effectively flat from the prior period's 16.1%. Growth edged down 0.08 points from 16.1% → 16.0%, remaining at the upper end of the historical range after accelerating from 11.0% in March 2025.
insights.implication: Growth has plateaued at the highest level in this window — the acceleration phase may be complete. If this holds, banks are sustaining a high-growth equilibrium rather than continuing to accelerate.

insights.title: Food credit surges to ₹1.04L Cr — new all-time high
insights.body: Food credit outstanding jumped to ₹103,915 crore in April 2026, the highest level on record. Volume spiked ₹70,271 Cr → ₹103,915 Cr, a ₹33,644 crore increase in one month, reversing the prior period's decline from ₹82,643 Cr.
insights.implication: Food credit has reversed its post-harvest decline and surged to a new peak — this is not typical seasonal behavior based on the available data. The move suggests either a policy-driven push or a structural shift in agricultural working capital demand.

insights.title: Food credit YoY decelerates to 66.4% from 139.4% peak
insights.body: Food credit grew 66.4% year-on-year in April 2026, down sharply from the prior period's 139.4%. Growth decelerated 139.4% → 66.4%, a 73 point drop, though it remains at the upper end of the historical range.
insights.implication: The explosive triple-digit growth has moderated, but food credit is still expanding at twice the system rate. The deceleration reflects the prior period's unsustainable spike normalizing, not a structural slowdown.

insights.title: Non-food FY26 addition hits ₹30.8L Cr — new record
insights.body: Non-food credit added ₹30.8 lakh crore during FY26, the maximum annual expansion in this dataset. The FY26 addition rose from ₹29.2L Cr → ₹30.8L Cr, an increase of ₹1.6 lakh crore over the prior reading.
insights.implication: This is the largest single-year non-food credit creation on record, accounting for 99% of total system expansion. Banks are channeling virtually all new lending into commercial and retail segments, not food-related financing — a clear signal of where institutional confidence and demand are concentrated.

insights.title: Non-food credit YoY holds at 15.8% — near upper range
insights.body: Non-food credit grew 15.8% year-on-year in April 2026, effectively flat from the prior period's 15.9%. Growth edged down 0.06 points from 15.9% → 15.8%, remaining at the upper end of the historical range after accelerating from 10.9% in March 2025.
insights.implication: Growth has plateaued at the highest level in this window — the acceleration phase appears complete. If this holds, banks are sustaining a high-growth equilibrium in non-food lending rather than continuing to accelerate.

gaps.title: Non-food credit at ₹211.1L Cr — first decline in 6 periods
gaps.body: Non-food credit fell to ₹211.1 lakh crore in April 2026, down ₹1.8 lakh crore from the March peak of ₹212.9 lakh crore. Volume contracted ₹212.9L Cr → ₹211.1L Cr, the first month-on-month decline after five consecutive months of expansion from ₹178.1L Cr in January 2025.
gaps.implication: Non-food credit drives 99% of system volume, so this decline mirrors the total bank credit reversal. The first contraction in six periods signals a potential inflection — watch whether this is a one-month correction or the start of a broader cooling trend.

insights.title: All 4 sectors growing YoY — breadth unchanged at maximum
insights.body: All 4 main sectors recorded positive year-on-year growth in April 2026, unchanged from the prior period. Breadth held steady at 4 sectors across all seven reporting periods from January 2025 through April 2026.
insights.implication: Growth remains broad-based across agriculture, industry, services, and retail — no sector is contracting or dragging down the overall expansion. This sustained breadth reinforces the structural nature of the credit cycle, not a narrow or fragile rally.

insights.title: Agriculture holds 12.44% of credit — stable near median
insights.body: Agriculture accounts for 12.44% of total bank credit in April 2026. Share ticked up marginally from 12.43% → 12.44%, essentially flat after the prior period's 0.12pp gain.
insights.implication: Agriculture maintains its traditional ~12.4% allocation despite double-digit growth — banks are expanding ag lending in lockstep with overall portfolio growth, not overweighting the sector.

insights.title: Agriculture growth slows to 13.7% — down from 15.7% peak
insights.body: Agriculture credit grew 13.70% year-on-year in April 2026. Growth decelerated from 15.70% → 13.70%, a -2.00pp pullback from the prior period's record high.
insights.implication: Agriculture has come off its peak but remains in the upper range of this window — the 15.7% spike was likely a one-period surge, now normalizing to a still-strong mid-teens pace.

insights.title: Industry FY acceleration hits 6.8pp — new record high
insights.body: Industry credit shows full-year acceleration of 6.8 percentage points in April 2026. FY acceleration edged up from 6.7pp → 6.8pp, breaking the six-period plateau at 6.7pp.
insights.implication: Industry lending momentum has strengthened further on a full-year basis — this is the highest acceleration on record, signaling sustained structural demand for industrial credit, not a cyclical blip.

insights.title: Industry share at 21.50% — lowest level in dataset
insights.body: Industry accounts for 21.50% of total credit in April 2026. Share edged down from 21.52% → 21.50%, now at the minimum level across all six periods.
insights.implication: Despite record growth rates, industry is losing share to faster-growing sectors — personal loans and services are outpacing industrial credit expansion, reshaping the portfolio mix.

insights.title: Industry credit at 15.06% YoY — highest growth on record
insights.body: Industry credit grew 15.06% year-on-year in April 2026, the strongest pace in this seven-period window. Growth accelerated from 14.96% → 15.06%, extending the upward trajectory from 8.2% in March 2025.
insights.implication: Industry credit is hitting peak momentum — likely driven by infrastructure investment, manufacturing recovery, and corporate capex revival as the economy scales productive capacity.

insights.title: Personal loans at 33.0% share — upper range, up 0.37pp
insights.body: Personal loans represent 32.98% of total credit in April 2026, the largest sector allocation. Share rose from 32.60% → 32.98%, reversing the prior period's 0.28pp decline and returning to the upper range.
insights.implication: Personal loans have regained share momentum after a brief dip — consumer lending is reasserting dominance in the credit mix, driven by sustained household demand.

insights.title: Personal loans grow 16.0% YoY — upper range, slight dip
insights.body: Personal loans credit grew 16.01% year-on-year in April 2026. Growth eased marginally from 16.24% → 16.01%, staying in the upper range after climbing from 11.7% in March 2025.
insights.implication: Consumer lending momentum remains strong despite the minor deceleration — household credit demand is durably positive, supporting continued retail-led portfolio expansion.

insights.title: Services FY acceleration holds at 7.0pp — maximum sustained
insights.body: Services credit maintained 7.0 percentage point full-year acceleration in April 2026. FY acceleration held steady at 7.0pp for seven consecutive periods since January 2025, the maximum level on record.
insights.implication: Services credit growth has structurally shifted higher and sustained the pace for over a year — this is an entrenched uptrend reflecting broad-based economic activity beyond manufacturing.

insights.title: Services share at 28.2% — down 0.26pp from peak
insights.body: Services captured 28.21% of total bank credit in April 2026. Share declined from 28.47% → 28.21%, pulling back from the prior period's record high but remaining in the upper range.
insights.implication: Services ceded share to personal loans this period but still hold near-peak allocation — the sector's structural uptrend is intact despite the minor pullback.

insights.title: Services credit at 18.6% YoY — second-highest in window
insights.body: Services credit grew 18.58% year-on-year in April 2026, down 0.41 percentage points from the prior period's 18.99% peak. Growth decelerated slightly from 18.99% → 18.58%, but remains near the upper end of the historical range and well above the 12.0% pace recorded in April 2025.
insights.implication: Services credit expansion remains robust at nearly double the historical norm, signaling sustained business investment and working capital demand. The marginal slowdown from the prior peak does not alter the structural growth story — this is still the economy's primary credit engine.

insights.title: Large corporates hold 67.2% of industry credit — stable at equilibrium
insights.body: Large corporates hold a 67.15% share of industry credit, up 0.01 points from the prior period. The share has stabilized at 67.1% → 67.2%, holding steady after the year-long decline from 71.0%.
insights.implication: The credit market has reached a new equilibrium. Large enterprises retain dominant access, but the stabilization confirms banks have completed their rebalancing toward smaller borrowers.

insights.title: Large corporate credit hits 10.0% YoY — strongest growth on record
insights.body: Large corporate credit growth accelerated to 10.04%, the strongest reading in this 7-period window. Growth momentum built from 8.9% → 10.0%, breaking above the prior maximum and extending the upward trajectory from 5.5% three periods ago.
insights.implication: Banks are backing large corporate capex plans with renewed confidence. This acceleration signals corporate India's investment cycle is gaining momentum with institutional financing support — the strongest signal yet.

insights.title: Medium enterprises at 9.7% share — highest level on record
insights.body: Medium enterprises hold a 9.73% share of industry credit, up 0.08 points from the prior period. The share climbed from 9.7% → 9.7%, holding at the maximum level after rising from 8.9% a year ago.
insights.implication: Medium enterprises have secured a steady 1-in-10 share of industry financing. The sustained maximum level confirms banks have locked in a higher allocation to this growth-critical segment.

insights.title: Micro & Small FY acceleration at 23.8pp — slight moderation from peak
insights.body: Micro & Small enterprise credit posted 23.8pp full-year acceleration, down 0.5 points from the prior period. Acceleration eased from 24.2pp → 23.8pp, the first decline after holding at maximum for six consecutive periods.
insights.implication: The small business lending surge remains entrenched but is beginning to normalize. The 23.8pp acceleration still signals systematic expansion of micro-enterprise exposure, not a temporary spike.

insights.title: Micro & Small at 23.1% share — holding near record high
insights.body: Micro & Small enterprises claimed 23.12% of total industry credit, down 0.08 points from the prior period. Share eased from 23.2% → 23.1%, holding near the maximum after climbing from 20.0% a year ago.
insights.implication: Small businesses have won a structurally larger slice of bank credit allocation. The sustained 23% level reflects deliberate portfolio rebalancing toward smaller ticket lending.

insights.title: Micro & Small credit at 30.1% YoY — decelerating from record peak
insights.body: Micro & Small enterprise credit surged 30.09% year-on-year, decelerating 3.05 points from the prior period. Growth eased from 33.1% → 30.1%, stepping back from the record high but still 3× the pace from a year ago (8.9%).
insights.implication: Banks are still aggressively expanding small business books, but the deceleration suggests the initial surge is normalizing. The 30% pace still signals a fundamental shift in credit allocation strategy.

insights.title: MSME-Large spread narrows to 20.1pp — gap closing from peak
insights.body: MSME credit outpaced large corporate lending by 20.1 percentage points, down 4.1 points from the prior period. The growth gap narrowed from 24.2pp → 20.1pp, driven by large corporate acceleration (8.9% → 10.0%) and MSME deceleration (33.1% → 30.1%).
insights.implication: Banks are rebalancing toward large corporates after months of MSME-heavy lending. The narrowing spread suggests either renewed confidence in large deals or normalization of the small-ticket surge — watch for further convergence.

gaps.title: Medium enterprise credit at 19.9% YoY — growth moderating from peak
gaps.body: Medium enterprise credit growth reached 19.88%, decelerating 1.86 points from the prior period. Growth momentum eased from 21.7% → 19.9%, stepping back from the recent peak but still outpacing large corporates by 10 points.
gaps.implication: Banks are still aggressively financing the missing middle, but the deceleration suggests the initial surge is normalizing. The 10-point premium over large corporates confirms sustained strategic focus on scale-up businesses.

insights.title: HFCs hold 17.9%, PFIs 16.9% — PFIs shift to active status
insights.body: Housing Finance Companies command 17.9% of NBFC credit while Public Financial Institutions hold 16.9%. PFIs shifted status from strengthening to active, with a 1.0 percentage point gap between the two segments.
insights.implication: The NBFC sector shows balanced allocation between housing and public finance, with PFIs stabilizing after recent momentum — HFCs maintain a narrow lead but the gap is tightening.

insights.title: PFIs surge 57.1% YoY — HFCs trail at 16.7%
insights.body: Public Financial Institutions are growing at 57.1% while Housing Finance Companies expand at 16.7%, both in strengthening status. A 40.4 percentage point growth spread separates the two NBFC segments, with PFIs accelerating from 52.5% in the prior period.
insights.implication: This is an extreme divergence — PFIs are in hypergrowth mode, likely reflecting government-backed lending programs or infrastructure finance, while HFCs face structural headwinds from regulatory tightening or market saturation.

insights.title: NBFCs lead at 34.5%, Trade weakens — status shifts signal rotation
insights.body: NBFCs command 34.5% of services credit, Trade holds 22.1%, and Other Services 20.5%. NBFCs shifted from strengthening to active status, while Trade moved from active to weakening — the spread between top and bottom remains 34.4 percentage points.
insights.implication: The NBFC boom is stabilizing after recent hypergrowth, while Trade is losing momentum — this signals a rotation within services credit toward financial intermediation and away from traditional commerce.

insights.title: Computer Software surged to 52.7% — now leads services growth
insights.body: Computer Software leads services growth at 52.7% and advanced to strengthening status, followed by Shipping at 40.7% and NBFCs at 27.7%. Computer Software jumped from 39.0% to 52.7% and shifted from active to strengthening. Shipping decelerated from 42.2% to 40.7% and moved back to active. Four other sub-sectors — Tourism, Hotels, Commercial Real Estate, and Trade — all shifted from strengthening to active.
insights.implication: Growth leadership has rotated sharply to technology, with Computer Software now the standout driver. The broad-based shift from strengthening to active across Tourism, Hotels, Real Estate, and Trade suggests the prior period's acceleration was a March year-end spike, not a sustained trend. The services expansion is now concentrating in tech and logistics rather than spreading evenly.

insights.title: Retail Trade share rises to 44.4% — wholesale loses ground
insights.body: Retail Trade holds 44.4% of trade credit and strengthened, while Wholesale Trade's 55.6% share weakened. The wholesale-retail split shifted toward retail, reversing the prior period's trend. Wholesale moved from strengthening to weakening; Retail moved from weakening to strengthening.
insights.implication: Consumer-facing retail is now capturing more credit allocation than B2B wholesale, a reversal from last period. This suggests end-consumer spending is driving trade credit more than supply chain financing — a shift worth monitoring if it persists beyond one period.

insights.title: Wholesale Trade at 18.1% YoY — double Retail's 8.0%
insights.body: Wholesale Trade grew 18.1% year-on-year and Retail Trade 8.0%, both holding active status despite the 10.1 percentage point growth gap. Wholesale decelerated from 20.1% to 18.1% and shifted from strengthening to active. Retail decelerated from 11.5% to 8.0% and also moved from strengthening to active.
insights.implication: Both wholesale and retail trade credit slowed from the prior period's strengthening pace, confirming the March spike was temporary. Wholesale still grows at more than double retail's rate, indicating businesses continue to invest in inventory and operations faster than consumers are spending — but the momentum has cooled across both channels.

insights.title: Credit cards sub-10% YoY for 4 periods — longest streak
insights.body: Credit card growth has stayed below 10% for 4 consecutive periods, the longest such streak on record. Streak extended from 3 periods to 4 periods, reaching a new maximum.
insights.implication: Credit card demand remains in a sustained weak phase with no recovery signal — consumer spending restraint is now entrenched, not cyclical.

insights.title: Credit cards at 4.28% share — marginal uptick from floor
insights.body: Credit cards now represent 4.28% of personal loans, up 0.04 percentage points from 4.24% last period. Share ticked up 4.24% → 4.28%, the first gain after hitting the lowest level in 6 periods.
insights.implication: Credit cards have stopped losing ground but remain near their weakest portfolio position — recovery is tentative at best.

insights.title: Credit cards 3.78% YoY — fourth period below 4%
insights.body: Credit card outstanding grew 3.78% year-on-year, up from 3.55% in the prior period. Growth edged up 3.55% → 3.78%, the second consecutive small gain after bottoming at 1.5%.
insights.implication: Credit cards are stabilising but remain in single-digit growth territory — the sector has not yet broken out of its post-collapse range.

insights.title: Education loans 2.24% share — lowest on record
insights.body: Education loans account for 2.24% of personal loans, down 0.01 percentage points from 2.25% last period. Share edged down 2.25% → 2.24%, reaching the minimum in this 7-period window.
insights.implication: Education loans maintain a small but stable share of personal lending, with slight erosion as faster-growing segments like gold loans gain ground.

insights.title: Education loans 13.27% YoY — slowest in this window
insights.body: Education loans grew 13.27% year-on-year, down 0.11 percentage points from 13.38% last period. Growth decelerated 13.38% → 13.27%, the slowest pace in this 7-period dataset.
insights.implication: Education loans remain the standout performer in personal lending with consistent double-digit growth, though momentum is gradually easing.

insights.title: FD advances 2.27% share — down 0.17pp from peak
insights.body: Advances against Fixed Deposits represent 2.27% of personal loans, down 0.17 percentage points from 2.44% last period. Share dropped 2.44% → 2.27%, retreating from the highest level in this window.
insights.implication: FD-backed advances have given back recent gains, suggesting the surge in collateralised lending may have been a one-period spike rather than a sustained shift.

insights.title: FD advances 9.61% YoY — collapsed from 19.22%
insights.body: FD-backed advances grew 9.61% year-on-year, down 9.61 percentage points from 19.22% last period. Growth halved 19.22% → 9.61%, the sharpest single-period deceleration in this dataset.
insights.implication: The aggressive expansion in collateralised personal lending has stalled — banks may be pulling back after a brief push into this safer product.

insights.title: Gold loans positive for 8 periods — longest run
insights.body: Gold loans have grown every period for 8 consecutive months, the longest unbroken run on record. Positive streak extended from 6 periods to 8 periods, marking a new maximum.
insights.implication: Gold lending has become the most reliable growth driver in personal loans with no signs of momentum breaking — the trend is entrenched.

insights.title: Gold loans 7.02% share — highest on record
insights.body: Gold loans captured 7.02% of all personal lending, up 0.39 percentage points from 6.63% last period. Share climbed 6.63% → 7.02%, reaching the highest level in this 7-period window.
insights.implication: Gold loans have more than doubled their portfolio weight in 12 months, reshaping the personal lending mix toward secured products at the expense of unsecured categories.

insights.title: Gold loans at 121.2% YoY — still doubling despite cooling
insights.body: Gold loan growth held at 121.21%, down marginally from 123.15% last period. Growth eased 123.15% → 121.21%, the second consecutive decline from the 128.8% peak in January 2026.
insights.implication: The explosive gold lending boom is moderating but remains in triple digits — banks are still doubling their gold portfolios year-on-year as borrowers pledge jewelry for liquidity.

insights.title: Housing share drops to 48.2% — lowest on record
insights.body: Housing loans fell to 48.15% of personal lending, the smallest share in this 7-period window. Portfolio weight declined 48.35% → 48.15%, extending the slide from over 50% a year ago.
insights.implication: Housing's structural dominance is eroding as gold and vehicle loans capture market share — the traditional anchor of personal credit is losing ground despite absolute growth.

insights.title: Housing growth steady at 11.4% — upper range sustained
insights.body: Housing loan growth held at 11.39%, essentially flat from 11.48% last period. Growth edged down 11.48% → 11.39%, remaining near the top of its 10.7-11.5% range.
insights.implication: Mortgage demand is resilient — housing credit is expanding at a healthy double-digit pace even as its portfolio share shrinks, indicating robust underlying volumes.

insights.title: Other Personal Loans at 24.9% — record low share
insights.body: Other personal loans held 24.90% of the portfolio, the smallest share in this dataset. Share slipped 24.99% → 24.90%, continuing the decline from 25.8% levels in early 2025.
insights.implication: Miscellaneous unsecured lending is losing ground to specialized secured products — banks are shifting mix toward collateral-backed exposure.

insights.title: Other Personal Loans at 12.7% YoY — momentum holding
insights.body: Other Personal Loans grew 12.70% year-on-year, down slightly from 12.98% last period. Growth eased 12.98% → 12.70%, still near the upper end of its 7.5-13.0% range.
insights.implication: Non-housing, non-vehicle personal credit is expanding at a solid pace despite losing share — absolute volumes are growing but slower than the overall personal loans pool.

insights.title: Securities-backed loans at 0.15% — minimal penetration
insights.body: Advances against shares and bonds held 0.15% of total personal loans, unchanged from last period. Share remained flat at 0.15%, fluctuating within a narrow 0.1-0.2% band across all periods.
insights.implication: Securities-backed lending remains a niche product with negligible market presence — banks are not scaling this segment despite recent growth volatility.

insights.title: Housing at 48.2%, Other PL 24.9%, Vehicle 10.7% — top three dominate
insights.body: Housing dominates personal loans at 48.2% share, followed by Other Personal Loans at 24.9% and Vehicle Loans at 10.7%. The top three categories control 83.8% of the market, with niche segments like advances against shares (0.1%) and consumer durables (0.3%) representing minimal share.
insights.implication: Personal credit remains concentrated in housing and general-purpose loans, with vehicle financing the only other material segment — product diversification is limited, and banks are doubling down on core secured categories.

insights.title: Vehicle loans at 10.7% share — stable in tight range
insights.body: Vehicle loans held 10.67% of personal loans, up marginally from 10.64% last period. Share edged up 10.64% → 10.67%, staying within the narrow 10.4-10.7% range.
insights.implication: Vehicle lending share is holding steady despite strong growth — the broader personal loans pool is expanding at a similar pace, keeping the mix stable.

insights.title: Vehicle loans at 18.0% YoY — strong growth sustained
insights.body: Vehicle loans grew 17.97% year-on-year, down slightly from 18.61% last period. Growth eased 18.61% → 17.97%, still near the top of its 8.6-18.6% range.
insights.implication: Auto financing remains in a pronounced upturn — growth has more than doubled from the 8.6% low a year ago, reflecting sustained consumer demand and aggressive bank competition in this secured segment.

gaps.title: Consumer durables negative for 6 periods — new record
gaps.body: Consumer durables lending has contracted for 6 consecutive periods, the longest negative streak on record. Negative streak jumped from 4 periods to 6 periods, extending the historical maximum by 2 periods.
gaps.implication: Consumer durables financing is in deep structural decline — discretionary spending on cars and appliances shows no sign of recovery.

gaps.title: Consumer durables flat at 0.32% — marginal segment
gaps.body: Consumer durables represent 0.32% of personal loans, unchanged from the prior period. Share held steady at 0.3%, near the lowest level in this dataset.
gaps.implication: Consumer durables have become a negligible segment within personal lending as sustained contraction erodes their portfolio weight.

gaps.title: Consumer durables -3.38% YoY — contraction easing
gaps.body: Consumer durables lending contracted 3.38% year-on-year, an improvement from the 5.34% decline last period. Contraction moderated -5.34% → -3.38%, the smallest decline in 4 periods.
gaps.implication: While still shrinking, consumer durables financing is declining at a slower pace — the worst may be behind, but growth has not returned.

gaps.title: Securities-backed loans turn negative at -3.6% — reversal confirmed
gaps.body: Advances against shares and bonds contracted 3.59% year-on-year, the first decline in this dataset. Growth reversed from +4.63% → -3.59%, an 8.2-point swing into contraction.
gaps.implication: Securities-backed lending has flipped from recovery to decline — the brief stabilization was short-lived, and banks are now pulling back from this volatile segment.

gaps.title: Gold 121.2%, Vehicle 18.0%, Education 13.3% lead — Consumer Durables -3.4%
gaps.body: Gold loans exploded 121.2% year-on-year, dwarfing all other personal loan categories, while Consumer Durables contracted 3.4% and Advances against shares fell 3.6%. The growth spread widened to 124.8 percentage points between gold loans and securities-backed advances, with Vehicle Loans (18.0%) and Education (13.3%) also showing strong momentum.
gaps.implication: Personal credit is polarizing sharply — secured lending against gold and vehicles is booming while unsecured consumer financing and securities-backed loans contract, reflecting banks' preference for tangible collateral amid economic uncertainty. Credit Cards flipped to strengthening at 3.8%, signaling a potential recovery in unsecured retail.

insights.title: PSL agriculture at 17.3% YoY — highest on record
insights.body: PSL agriculture credit grew 17.33% year-on-year in April 2026, the strongest pace in this 7-period window. Growth accelerated from 16.03% → 17.33%, extending a steady climb from 11.0% in March 2025.
insights.implication: Rural credit demand is strengthening as agricultural income improves and banks expand seasonal working capital and farm mechanisation lending. The sustained acceleration signals structural improvement in farm sector creditworthiness.

insights.title: PSL education loans at 23.3% YoY — holding near peak
insights.body: PSL educational loans expanded 23.27% year-on-year, maintaining near-peak growth with minimal change from the prior period. Growth edged up from 23.02% → 23.27%, stabilising after surging from 0.9% a year ago.
insights.implication: Student loan demand remains robust as higher education costs rise, with banks sustaining aggressive expansion despite the rapid run-up. The plateau suggests underwriting discipline is now balancing growth appetite.

insights.title: PSL housing at 39.9% YoY — record growth sustained
insights.body: PSL housing credit surged 39.94% year-on-year, holding at the highest growth rate in this window. Growth edged up from 39.80% → 39.94%, sustaining the dramatic turnaround from -1.1% a year ago.
insights.implication: Affordable housing demand is booming as interest rates stabilise and government incentives drive first-time buyer activity. The sustained near-40% pace signals structural shift in priority sector housing penetration.

insights.title: PSL medium enterprises at 17.2% YoY — slowest on record
insights.body: PSL medium enterprise credit grew 17.24% year-on-year, marking the weakest pace in this 7-period window. Growth decelerated from 17.85% → 17.24%, down from peaks above 22% in early 2025.
insights.implication: Mid-market businesses are facing tighter credit conditions as banks shift focus to retail and micro-enterprise lending with better risk profiles. The sustained deceleration suggests structural reallocation away from medium enterprises.

insights.title: PSL MSME acceleration at 15.9pp — first decline from peak
insights.body: PSL MSME credit acceleration measured 15.9 percentage points on a full-year basis, the first reading below the prior 16.1pp maximum. Acceleration eased from 16.1pp → 15.9pp after holding steady for six consecutive periods.
insights.implication: MSME lending momentum remains structurally elevated but is no longer accelerating at the peak rate. The shift suggests the explosive growth phase driven by government guarantees and digital platforms is maturing into sustained high-growth equilibrium.

insights.title: PSL micro & small at 28.5% YoY — cooling from peak
insights.body: PSL micro and small enterprise credit expanded 28.53% year-on-year, easing from the prior period's record high. Growth decelerated from 29.53% → 28.53%, though still more than double the 13.4% level a year ago.
insights.implication: Small business credit remains in a lending boom driven by digital platforms and government guarantees, but the first deceleration from peak suggests banks are moderating pace after the rapid run-up. Watch for sustained cooling as a signal of tightening underwriting.

insights.title: PSL renewable energy positive for 8 straight periods — record streak
insights.body: Renewable energy lending has grown for 8 consecutive periods, the longest positive streak on record. Extended from 6 to 8 periods, reaching a new historical maximum.
insights.implication: Banks are sustaining their renewable energy push despite recent growth moderation, signaling structural commitment to green financing mandates. The unbroken streak confirms this is not a cyclical play but a permanent portfolio shift.

insights.title: PSL renewable energy at 15.6% YoY — sharp deceleration to lowest
insights.body: Renewable energy lending grew 15.64%, down sharply from 34.13% in the prior period. Growth decelerated by 18.49 points, falling to the weakest reading in this 7-period window after peaking at 78.3% in March 2025.
insights.implication: The green lending boom is cooling rapidly after explosive growth phases, though double-digit expansion suggests the sector remains structurally attractive. The sharp deceleration signals banks are normalising after front-loading renewable energy exposure to meet mandates.

insights.title: PSL social infrastructure at 35.3% YoY — surging after volatility
insights.body: Social infrastructure lending grew 35.27%, surging from 12.88% in the prior period. Growth accelerated by 22.39 points, swinging from -1.0% contraction in February 2026 to 35.3% expansion.
insights.implication: Banks are aggressively re-engaging with social infrastructure after volatile swings, likely driven by government spending on healthcare and education facilities. The sharp acceleration suggests lumpy project disbursements rather than steady trend — watch for reversal.

insights.title: PSL weaker sections at 10.7% YoY — weakest on record
insights.body: Weaker sections lending grew 10.67%, the slowest pace in this 7-period window. Growth decelerated from 11.07% → 10.67%, down from 15.3% in March 2025.
insights.implication: Financial inclusion momentum is softening as banks prioritize higher-yield PSL categories over mandated weaker section targets. The sustained deceleration signals structural reallocation toward agriculture, MSME, and housing segments with better risk-return profiles.

insights.title: Housing 39.9%, Export Credit -12.0% — PSL spread widens to 52.4%
insights.body: Housing leads PSL growth at 39.9%, while export credit contracts 12.0% and others decline 12.5%. Spread widened from 49.4% to 52.4%. Export credit deteriorated from -8.4% → -12.0%, others from -9.6% → -12.5%. Micro and Small Enterprises shifted from strengthening to active status at 28.5%, Educational Loans moved from active to strengthening.
insights.implication: PSL bifurcation is intensifying — housing and micro enterprises remain the only bankable priority segments while trade-linked categories are in structural decline. The 52.4% spread signals banks are concentrating capital in proven retail/MSME plays and abandoning export-linked exposures. Micro and Small Enterprises crossing into active status at 28.5% confirms this is now a core growth engine, not a compliance play.

gaps.title: PSL export credit at -12.0% YoY — decline re-widening
gaps.body: PSL export credit contracted 12.02% year-on-year, reversing the prior period's improvement. Contraction widened from -8.36% → -12.02%, breaking the recovery trend from the -17.2% trough in January 2026.
gaps.implication: Export financing remains under pressure from global trade headwinds. The reversal after three months of improvement suggests banks see no near-term export recovery, raising caution on trade-linked working capital exposure.

gaps.title: PSL others at -12.5% YoY — contraction re-widening
gaps.body: PSL other categories contracted 12.45% year-on-year, reversing the prior period's sharp improvement. Contraction widened from -9.55% → -12.45%, breaking the recovery trend from the -23.9% trough in January 2026.
gaps.implication: Specialty PSL categories are volatile as banks complete portfolio rebalancing. The reversal after improvement suggests no sustained recovery — banks remain focused on core agriculture, MSME, and housing segments.

insights.title: Iron & Steel share slips to 68.2% — Other Metal gains ground
insights.body: Iron & Steel holds 68.2% of basic metal credit while Other Metal Products commands 31.8%. Iron & Steel's share declined from 68.8% → 68.2% while Other Metal Products rose from 31.2% → 31.8%, narrowing the spread from 37.7 to 36.4 percentage points.
insights.implication: The share shift confirms specialty metals are steadily capturing allocation from traditional steel, though infrastructure-driven steel lending still dominates. Watch for continued rotation toward higher-value metal products.

insights.title: Other Metal Products 19.9% YoY — outpacing Iron & Steel at 16.7%
insights.body: Other Metal Products grew 19.9% year-on-year versus Iron & Steel at 16.7%. Growth rates converged — the spread narrowed from 2.2 to 3.3 percentage points, with Other Metal Products accelerating from 21.0% → 19.9% and Iron & Steel decelerating from 18.7% → 16.7%.
insights.implication: Despite share dominance, Iron & Steel growth is slowing while specialty metals maintain momentum. This divergence signals banks are backing downstream metal fabrication and specialty alloys over core steel production.

insights.title: Pharma share dips to 33.5% — Fertiliser stabilises at 10.2%
insights.body: Others leads chemical credit at 44.1%, followed by Drugs & Pharmaceuticals at 33.5%, Petro Chemicals at 12.2%, and Fertiliser at 10.2%. Drugs & Pharmaceuticals share declined from 33.9% → 33.5% while Fertiliser rose from 9.9% → 10.2%, with the spread narrowing from 34.3 to 33.8 percentage points.
insights.implication: Pharma's share erosion and fertiliser's stabilisation suggest a pause in the prior rotation. All segments now show balanced positioning — no clear winner emerging in chemical credit allocation this period.

insights.title: Electronics share jumps to 19.9% — Other Engineering slips to 80.1%
insights.body: Other Engineering holds 80.1% of engineering credit while Electronics commands 19.9%. Electronics share rose from 18.9% → 19.9% while Other Engineering declined from 81.1% → 80.1%, narrowing the spread from 62.2 to 60.2 percentage points.
insights.implication: Electronics is clawing back share from traditional engineering for the first time in this window. The 1 percentage point shift signals early rotation toward tech hardware manufacturing over heavy machinery.

insights.title: Other Engineering 34.5% YoY — Electronics strengthens to 17.8%
insights.body: Other Engineering grows 34.5% year-on-year versus Electronics at 17.8%. Other Engineering decelerated from 37.5% → 34.5% while Electronics accelerated from 13.3% → 17.8%, narrowing the spread from 24.2 to 16.7 percentage points.
insights.implication: Electronics growth is re-accelerating while heavy engineering cools. The narrowing gap confirms banks are beginning to back electronics manufacturing — likely driven by PLI schemes and domestic semiconductor push.

insights.title: Edible Oils share rises to 11.1% — Sugar slips to 11.1%
insights.body: Others commands 75.9% of food processing credit, followed by Sugar at 11.1%, Edible Oils at 11.1%, and Tea at 1.9%. Edible Oils share rose from 10.4% → 11.1% while Sugar declined from 11.9% → 11.1%, with the spread narrowing from 73.7 to 74.0 percentage points.
insights.implication: Edible Oils has overtaken Sugar in share allocation for the first time. This marks a structural shift toward processed food inputs over traditional commodity crops in food processing credit.

insights.title: Infrastructure 32.8% share — Basic Metal 11.3%, Other Industries 7.5%
insights.body: Infrastructure dominates industry credit with a 32.8% share, followed by Basic Metal and Metal Products at 11.3% and Other Industries at 7.5%. Infrastructure share rose from 32.6% → 32.8% while Basic Metal held steady at 11.3%. The top 3 segments control over half of industry lending, with bottom segments like Leather at 0.3% and Glass at 0.4% unchanged.
insights.implication: Infrastructure continues to consolidate its credit dominance — banks are doubling down on India's capital formation story. The concentration is intensifying, not diversifying.

insights.title: Petroleum 37.8% YoY leads — All Engineering 30.8%, Gems 26.0%
insights.body: Petroleum, Coal Products and Nuclear Fuels leads industry growth at 37.8%, followed by All Engineering at 30.8% and Gems & Jewellery at 26.0%. Growth remains broad-based with 9 active and 10 strengthening sectors. Petroleum accelerated from 32.5% → 37.8% while Gems decelerated from 41.4% → 26.0%. The spread narrowed from 31.3 to 31.3 percentage points.
insights.implication: Energy transition financing is accelerating — Petroleum's surge signals banks are backing India's refining and nuclear capacity expansion. Gems cooled but remains strong, confirming sustained export-oriented lending across multiple sectors.

insights.title: Power 56.8% share — Roads 22.8%, Other Infrastructure 12.2%
insights.body: Power commands 56.8% of infrastructure credit, with Roads at 22.8% and Other Infrastructure at 12.2%. Power share rose from 56.6% → 56.8% while Roads rose from 22.6% → 22.8%. The top 3 infrastructure categories control 91.8% of the segment, with transport modes like Airports at 0.5% and Railways at 0.4% unchanged.
insights.implication: Power and Roads are tightening their grip on infrastructure credit — the concentration is intensifying. Banks are backing core energy and connectivity over diversified transport infrastructure.

insights.title: Other Textiles dominates at 44.7% — Cotton second at 36.5%
insights.body: Other Textiles dominates at 44.7% of textile credit, followed by Cotton Textiles at 36.5%. All four textile sub-segments remain active with a 42.9% spread between largest (Other Textiles) and smallest (Jute Textiles at 1.7%).
insights.implication: Textile lending is concentrated in non-cotton segments, likely reflecting synthetic fiber and technical textile expansion over traditional cotton manufacturing.

insights.title: Jute Textiles leads at 19.9% YoY — all four segments growing
insights.body: All four textile segments posted positive growth, led by Jute Textiles at 19.9% and Other Textiles at 13.3%. Broad-based growth with Jute Textiles and Cotton Textiles both strengthening, while Other Textiles and Man-Made Textiles shifted from strengthening to active.
insights.implication: Even traditional manufacturing sectors like textiles are benefiting from the credit expansion. The universal positive growth signals broad industrial optimism, though the status downgrades in Other and Man-Made suggest momentum is moderating in the larger segments.

gaps.title: Pharma surges 20.6% YoY — Fertiliser flat at -0.3%
gaps.body: Drugs & Pharmaceuticals leads chemical growth at 20.6%, followed by Petro Chemicals at 19.4% and Others at 16.4%, while Fertiliser contracts -0.3%. Pharma accelerated from 17.9% → 20.6% while Petro Chemicals decelerated sharply from 27.7% → 19.4%. Fertiliser improved from -4.5% → -0.3%, narrowing the spread from 32.2 to 20.9 percentage points.
gaps.implication: Pharma has seized growth leadership as petrochemicals cool. Fertiliser's contraction is moderating — near stabilisation after sustained decline. Banks are rotating toward pharma manufacturing and away from commodity chemicals.

highlight: Petroleum, Coal Products and Nuclear Fuels

gaps.title: Edible Oils 33.0% YoY — Sugar turns negative at -0.4%
gaps.body: Edible Oils & Vanaspati leads food processing growth at 33.0%, followed by Others at 13.6%, while Sugar contracts -0.4% and Tea -3.1%. Edible Oils accelerated from 23.9% → 33.0% while Others decelerated from 14.8% → 13.6%. Sugar turned negative from 4.3% → -0.4% and Tea from 0.8% → -3.1%, widening the spread from 23.1 to 36.1 percentage points.
gaps.implication: Edible Oils is surging while traditional commodities collapse. Sugar's first contraction and Tea's accelerating decline signal banks are abandoning legacy agri-processing for value-added food manufacturing.

gaps.title: Ports surge 74.0% YoY — Power 21.5%, Railways collapse -44.1%
gaps.body: Ports surge 74.0% while Power grows 21.5% and Other Infrastructure 1.4%. Railways contracts -44.1%, Airports -26.0%, and Telecommunications -11.9%. Ports accelerated from 48.7% → 74.0% while Power decelerated from 22.1% → 21.5%. Railways worsened from -50.1% → -44.1% while Airports improved from -27.2% → -26.0%. The spread widened from 98.8 to 118.1 percentage points.
gaps.implication: Ports financing is exploding — banks are aggressively backing India's export infrastructure as trade volumes surge. Traditional transport sectors remain in deep contraction, confirming the structural shift toward trade-enabling over domestic connectivity infrastructure.
